mitre_attack: T1562.001 - Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools
cwe: CWE-444 - Inconsistent Interpretation of HTTP Requests (HTTP Request Smuggling)
CWE-345 - Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity

threat.title: HTTP Request Smuggling via Content-Length and Transfer-Encoding Desynchronization
threat.description: HTTP Request Smuggling exploits inconsistent interpretation of HTTP request boundaries between front-end (reverse proxy/load balancer) and back-end servers. When these systems disagree on where one request ends and another begins—typically due to conflicting Content-Length (CL) and Transfer-Encoding (TE) headers—attackers can smuggle malicious requests that bypass security controls, poison caches, hijack user sessions, and gain unauthorized access to sensitive resources.

attack.scenario:
1. Attacker identifies architecture with front-end reverse proxy (Nginx) and back-end application server (Gunicorn/Node.js)
2. Crafts malicious request with both Content-Length and Transfer-Encoding headers that are interpreted differently by front-end vs back-end
3. Front-end processes request using Content-Length, forwarding what it considers a complete request
4. Back-end uses Transfer-Encoding (chunked), leaving unprocessed bytes that become prefix of next legitimate user's request
5. Smuggled request prefix poisons the next user's request, bypassing authentication, ACLs, or WAF rules
6. Attacker gains access to admin endpoints (/admin), steals session tokens, or executes stored XSS against other users
prerequisites: Architecture with front-end proxy/load balancer and back-end application server
Front-end and back-end systems disagree on Content-Length vs Transfer-Encoding priority
HTTP/1.1 protocol in use (not HTTP/2 end-to-end)
Connection reuse/keep-alive enabled between proxy and back-end

cve_references.description: ASP.NET Core HTTP Request Smuggling via chunk extensions. Microsoft assigned CVSS 9.9, their highest severity for ASP.NET Core to date. Vulnerability allows authorized attacker to bypass security features over network via inconsistent interpretation of chunked transfer encoding.

Gunicorn 21.2.0 HTTP Request/Response Smuggling (TE.CL). Improper validation of Transfer-Encoding header leads to fallback to Content-Length, enabling TE.CL smuggling attacks. Affects cache poisoning, session hijacking, SSRF, XSS, and security bypass scenarios.

Akamai HTTP Request Smuggling via OPTIONS method with obsolete line folding. Vulnerability occurred with HTTP/1.x OPTIONS requests containing Expect: 100-continue header using obsolete line folding syntax, allowing request boundary confusion.

real_world_breaches.incident: Apple Core Web Applications Request Smuggling
real_world_breaches.impact: Apple's core web applications found vulnerable to HTTP Request Smuggling via CL.TE issues. Front-end and back-end servers disagreed on HTTP header processing, potentially allowing attackers to bypass authentication and access controls.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Inconsistent Content-Length and Transfer-Encoding interpretation between reverse proxy and application servers

real_world_breaches.impact: Bug bounty researcher identified HTTP Request Smuggling affecting Akamai CDN infrastructure. Vulnerability quickly patched via platform-wide fix with no evidence of exploitation. Demonstrated that even major CDN providers remain susceptible to request smuggling variants.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Improper handling of obsolete line folding in HTTP OPTIONS requests with Expect headers

bug_bounty_reports.summary: HTTP Request Smuggling via OPTIONS + obsolete line folding affecting Akamai CDN platform

security_research.title: HTTP Request Smuggling: What is it and How to Prevent it
security_research.key_finding: Request smuggling attacks exploit inconsistent interpretation between front-end and back-end servers, with CL.TE and TE.CL being the two primary attack variants. Attackers can bypass security controls, poison caches, and compromise other users.

security_research.title: HTTP Request Smuggling in 2024: New Variants and Cloud Provider Vulnerabilities
security_research.key_finding: Research in 2024 uncovered TE.0 smuggling variant where back-end servers ignore Transfer-Encoding entirely, treating message body length as zero. Thousands of Google Cloud-hosted websites found vulnerable to this technique.

security_research.title: CVE-2025-32094: HTTP Request Smuggling Via OPTIONS + Obsolete Line Folding
security_research.key_finding: Modern CDN platforms remain vulnerable to HTTP request smuggling when combining obsolete HTTP/1.1 features (line folding) with specific methods (OPTIONS) and headers (Expect: 100-continue), demonstrating continued relevance of historical protocol quirks.

mitigations.title: Enforce HTTP/2 End-to-End with Protocol Downgrade Prevention
mitigations.description: HTTP/2 multiplexing eliminates request smuggling by using binary framing and strict message boundaries. Deploy HTTP/2 from client to front-end AND front-end to back-end, disabling HTTP/1.1 fallback where possible. For environments requiring HTTP/1.1, enforce HTTP/2 at least between proxy and back-end.
iso27001_controls: A.14.1.2 - Securing application services on public networks
nist_csf: PR.PT-4 - Communications and control networks are protected
validation_steps: Verify HTTP/2 is enabled: curl -I --http2 https://api.example.com
Test rejection of dual headers: Send request with both Content-Length and Transfer-Encoding
Confirm HTTP/1.1 fallback is blocked or properly normalized
Use smuggler-detector.py or http-request-smuggler Burp extension to scan for vulnerabilities
Monitor Nginx error logs for 400 Bad Request responses from header validation

mitigations.title: Implement Request Normalization and Header Validation
mitigations.description: Deploy middleware or proxy layer that strictly validates and normalizes HTTP requests before forwarding. Reject requests with conflicting headers (CL + TE), multiple identical headers, or malformed chunked encoding. Use RFC 7230/7231 compliant parsers.

code_example.code:
# HAProxy configuration for request smuggling prevention

global
    log stdout local0
    maxconn 4096
    ssl-default-bind-ciphers ECDHE-ECDSA-AES128-GCM-SHA256:ECDHE-RSA-AES128-GCM-SHA256
    ssl-default-bind-options ssl-min-ver TLSv1.2

defaults
    log     global
    mode    http
    option  httplog
    option  dontlognull
    timeout connect 5s
    timeout client  30s
    timeout server  30s
    
    # Strict HTTP compliance
    option http-strict-errors
    
frontend http_frontend
    bind *:443 ssl crt /etc/haproxy/certs/cert.pem alpn h2,http/1.1
    
    # Enable HTTP/2
    option forwardfor
    
    # ACLs for request smuggling detection
    acl has_content_length hdr(content-length) -m found
    acl has_transfer_encoding hdr(transfer-encoding) -m found
    acl multiple_content_length hdr_cnt(content-length) gt 1
    acl multiple_transfer_encoding hdr_cnt(transfer-encoding) gt 1
    acl invalid_transfer_encoding hdr(transfer-encoding) -m reg -i '(chunked.*chunked|chunked.+,|,.+chunked)'
    acl has_both_headers and has_content_length has_transfer_encoding
    
    # Reject malicious requests
    http-request deny deny_status 400 if has_both_headers
    http-request deny deny_status 400 if multiple_content_length
    http-request deny deny_status 400 if multiple_transfer_encoding
    http-request deny deny_status 400 if invalid_transfer_encoding
    
    # Normalize headers
    http-request del-header Transfer-Encoding if has_content_length
    
    # Add security headers
    http-response set-header X-Frame-Options DENY
    http-response set-header X-Content-Type-Options nosniff
    
    default_backend app_servers

backend app_servers
    balance roundrobin
    option httpchk GET /health HTTP/1.1\r\nHost:\ api.example.com
    
    # Disable connection reuse to prevent smuggling attacks
    # Option 1: Use Connection: close (more secure, less efficient)
    http-request set-header Connection close
    
    # Option 2: Or use short keepalive with validation (more efficient)
    # option http-server-close
    # timeout http-keep-alive 1s
    
    server app1 10.0.1.10:8080 check inter 2s rise 2 fall 3
    server app2 10.0.1.11:8080 check inter 2s rise 2 fall 3

# Statistics endpoint (restrict access in production)
listen stats
    bind *:8404
    stats enable
    stats uri /stats
    stats refresh 30s
    stats auth admin:secure_password_here

nist_csf: PR.DS-5 - Protections against data leaks are implemented
DE.CM-1 - The network is monitored to detect potential cybersecurity events
validation_steps: Test CL.TE attack: Send request with Content-Length: 6 and Transfer-Encoding: chunked with smuggled payload
Test TE.CL attack: Send request with Transfer-Encoding: chunked and Content-Length mismatch
Verify HAProxy rejects requests with both headers: Check for HTTP 400 responses
Use Burp Suite HTTP Request Smuggler extension to scan endpoint
Monitor HAProxy logs for denied requests: grep 'deny' /var/log/haproxy.log

mitigations.title: Configure AWS Application Load Balancer with Security Best Practices
mitigations.description: AWS ALB provides built-in protections against request smuggling when properly configured. Enable HTTP/2, use target groups with health checks, configure CloudWatch alarms for anomalies, and apply AWS WAF rules to detect and block smuggling attempts.
nist_csf: PR.PT-4 - Communications and control networks are protected
DE.CM-1 - The network is monitored to detect potential cybersecurity events
validation_steps: Verify ALB has drop_invalid_header_fields = true in configuration
Confirm HTTP/2 is enabled: curl --http2 -I https://your-alb-dns.com
Test WAF rules by sending request with both CL and TE headers (should receive 400)
Check CloudWatch metrics for WAF blocks: AWS Console → WAF → Web ACLs → Metrics
Review ALB access logs in S3 for suspicious patterns: aws s3 ls s3://alb-logs-bucket/
Set up CloudWatch alarms for abnormal traffic patterns and WAF block rates

indicators: Unexpected 400 Bad Request errors from reverse proxy/load balancer
Requests with both Content-Length and Transfer-Encoding headers in logs
Multiple or malformed Transfer-Encoding headers (e.g., 'chunked, chunked' or 'chunked, identity')
Discrepancies between logged request size and actual bytes received
Unauthorized access to admin endpoints from unexpected source IPs
Session hijacking: Same session token used from multiple IPs simultaneously
Cache poisoning: Users receiving responses meant for other users
Time-based anomalies: Delayed responses indicating request queue poisoning

log_queries.query:
// Detect requests with both Content-Length and Transfer-Encoding headers
sequence by source.ip, http.request.id
  [network where event.category == "web" and 
   http.request.headers.content-length != null and 
   http.request.headers.transfer-encoding == "chunked"]
  [network where event.category == "web" and 
   http.response.status_code == 400 or http.response.status_code == 403]
| where _arraysearch(network.headers, header, header.name == "content-length" and header.name == "transfer-encoding")

index=proxy_logs sourcetype=nginx:access OR sourcetype=haproxy:http
| rex field=http_headers "(?i)content-length:\s*(?<content_length>\d+)"
| rex field=http_headers "(?i)transfer-encoding:\s*(?<transfer_encoding>\w+)"
| where isnotnull(content_length) AND isnotnull(transfer_encoding)
| eval smuggling_attempt="Dual Headers Detected"
| stats count by src_ip, host, method, uri, content_length, transfer_encoding, status, _time
| where count > 0
| sort -count

// Detect HTTP Request Smuggling attempts in Azure Application Gateway logs
AzureDiagnostics
| where Category == "ApplicationGatewayAccessLog"
| extend RequestHeaders = parse_json(requestHeaders_s)
| where RequestHeaders.['Content-Length'] != "" and RequestHeaders.['Transfer-Encoding'] contains "chunked"
| project TimeGenerated, clientIP_s, host_s, requestUri_s, httpMethod_s, httpStatus_d, RequestHeaders
| extend SmugglingIndicator = "Both CL and TE headers present"
| summarize AttemptsCount=count() by clientIP_s, host_s, bin(TimeGenerated, 5m)
| where AttemptsCount > 1
| order by AttemptsCount desc

// Detect malformed Transfer-Encoding headers
http.request.headers.transfer-encoding: (*chunked*chunked* OR *chunked*,* OR *,*chunked*) 
OR (http.request.headers.content-length: * AND http.request.headers.transfer-encoding: chunked)
| stats count() by source.ip, url.path, http.request.headers.transfer-encoding
| where count > 0

// Request smuggling detection with time-based anomalies
_sourceCategory=proxy/nginx OR _sourceCategory=alb/access
| parse regex "(?<method>\w+)\s+(?<uri>\S+)\s+HTTP/[\d\.]+" nodrop
| parse regex "Content-Length:\s*(?<cl>\d+)" nodrop
| parse regex "Transfer-Encoding:\s*(?<te>\w+)" nodrop
| where !isEmpty(cl) and !isEmpty(te)
| count by src_ip, uri, method, status
| where _count > 5

# Query for ALB access logs detecting request smuggling patterns
fields @timestamp, clientIp, request, requestProcessingTime, targetProcessingTime, responseCode, sentBytes
| filter request like /Content-Length/ and request like /Transfer-Encoding/
| stats count() as attempts by clientIp, request
| filter attempts > 3
| sort attempts desc

tools.name: PortSwigger HTTP Request Smuggler (Burp Suite)
tools.usage: Automated detection extension for Burp Suite that tests CL.TE, TE.CL, and TE.TE variants

tools.name: AWS GuardDuty
tools.usage: Monitors for malicious activity including HTTP smuggling attempts in AWS environments
